home.title: Explore the Cosmos
home.subtitle: Join the Uzbek space program and embark on an interactive journey through the universe.
home.academySubtitle: Our core mission is education. We provide structured, interactive courses designed to take you from a beginner to a space expert.
home.curriculumDesc: Follow a carefully designed learning path. Start with the basics of the solar system and advance to orbital mechanics and astrophysics.
home.missionsDesc: Learning isn't just reading. Engage with 3D simulations, interactive quizzes, and real-world problem-solving scenarios.
home.certDesc: Complete units to earn XP, level up your profile, and build a verifiable portfolio of your space engineering knowledge.
home.highlightLiveDesc: Watch live NASA and SpaceX events
home.platformBadge: Space edu — learn space science with clarity

uzSpace.title: Uzbekistan's
uzSpace.subtitle: From the ancient observatories of Samarkand to the future of satellite engineering, Uzbekistan has always looked to the stars. Join us as we build the next chapter.
uzSpace.ulughBeg: Mirzo Ulug'bek (Mīrzā Muḥammad Ṭarāghāy ibn Shāhrukh)
uzSpace.ulughBegRole: Timurid prince & astronomer; ruler of Mavarannahr (Transoxiana) from Samarkand
uzSpace.ulughBegDesc: Grandson of Amir Timur (Tamerlane), he governed Transoxiana and made Samarkand a capital of exact science. His observatory and Zij-i Sultani tied Uzbekistan’s name to global star-mapping and calendar precision.
uzSpace.ulughBegHistory:
Historical depth: Mirzo Ulug'bek (1394–1449) governed Transoxiana (Mavarannahr) as a Timurid prince-scientist. Around 1424 he founded the Samarkand Observatory on the Kuhak hill; its monumental meridian arc (about 11 metres along the rim of a sextant-scale instrument) let astronomers measure zenith distances of the Sun and stars with far smaller error than typical portable instruments. Scholars such as Ghiyath al-Din al-Kashi (until his death in 1429) and later Ali Qushji refined instruments and mathematics on site.

The observatory’s main legacy is the Zij-i Sultani (also called Zij-i Guragani)—a handbook of tables and a star catalogue listing 1,018 stars with ecliptic coordinates, together with a refined value for the obliquity of the ecliptic (~23°30'). Copies spread to Istanbul, Dehli, and Europe via Persian and Arabic manuscripts, influencing astronomical practice for centuries. Ulug'bek was killed in 1449 in a princely coup, but his data survived in manuscript tradition as a symbol of Central Asian science.
uzSpace.ulughBegPoints: Timurid statecraft + science: patronage of madrasas and observatory as one project|1424–1429: Samarkand Observatory with a large meridian instrument for fixed-site precision|Collaboration with al-Kashi on trigonometry and π for instrument design|Zij-i Sultani: 1,018 stars, solar & planetary tables used by later Muslim and Indian astronomers|Latitude of Samarkand fixed in the zij for local timekeeping and calendar reform|After 1449: observatory partly dismantled, but tables copied across Persianate world and studied in Europe
uzSpace.farghaniRole: Abū al-ʿAbbās Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad ibn Kathīr al-Farghānī — astronomer & mathematician; Ferghana Valley → Abbasid Baghdad
uzSpace.farghaniDesc: From the Ferghana region in Central Asia (today’s Uzbekistan), he distilled Ptolemaic and early Islamic astronomy into clear manuals. Latin translations shaped medieval universities and linked his work to later tables from Cairo to Samarqand.
uzSpace.farghaniHistory:
Historical depth: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Kathir al-Farghani (Latin: Alfraganus, d. c. 861) came from the Ferghana Valley in Central Asia and worked in Abbasid Baghdad—then the largest scientific city in the world. His Kitab fi harakat al-nujum (“Book on celestial motions,” often Latinized) gave a clear, ordered account of the Ptolemaic cosmos: epicycles, eccentrics, lunar parallax, eclipses, and a famous estimate of Earth’s diameter by comparing arc length to shadow angles.

Latin translations (12th–13th c., e.g. via translators such as John of Seville) became standard university texts alongside Sacrobosco, and parameters from this tradition echoed for centuries in geographical handbooks—one route by which Central Asian astronomy entered wider debates about measuring the Earth. Al-Farg‘oniy also wrote on the sundial, astrolabe, and armillary ring, and his star lore helped unify Arabic star names with Greek constellations for later Islamic observatories—including the intellectual line that would reach Samarkand under Ulug'bek.
uzSpace.farghaniPoints: Ferghana → Baghdad: Central Asian scholar in the heart of Abbasid science|Summarized Ptolemy for students: planets, spheres, eclipses, parallax|Earth’s size: geometric method using one degree of meridian arc|Medieval Latin “Alfraganus”: backbone of European astronomy courses before Copernicus|Instruments: sundials, astrolabes, rings—practical astronomy for mosques & navigation|Long reception: his parameters echoed in later zij tables from Cairo to Samarqand
uzSpace.obsDesc: Construction of the most advanced observatory of the medieval world in Samarkand.
uzSpace.agencyDesc: Establishment of 'Uzbekkosmos' to regulate and develop space activities in Uzbekistan.
uzSpace.hubDesc: Launch of the first national satellite engineering laboratory for students.
uzSpace.lunarDesc: Uzbek scientists contribute to international lunar base construction projects.
uzSpace.astronautDesc: A historic moment as the first citizen of Uzbekistan travels to the International Space Station.
uzSpace.visionDesc: To position Uzbekistan as a leader in Central Asian space technology, fostering a new generation of engineers and scientists.
uzSpace.joinDesc: Start your training today and become part of Uzbekistan's cosmic future.

game.introDesc: Survive the asteroid field, collect glowing energy cores, and push distance. Arrows or WASD steer, hold Shift or Space for boost. Cores restore health/boost, and power-ups grant shield, slow-mo, or magnet.
game.shopDesc: Spend arcade stars ★ (earned from coins & survival). Equipped: {{skin}}
game.controlsDesc: Hold Shift/Space to boost · blue shield · purple slow · green magnet

lesson.lessonDescription: In this lesson you will deeply explore {lessonName}. The visualizations and explanations provided in the video will help you easily grasp complex concepts. By the end of this lesson, you will be one step closer to the secrets of the universe.
lesson.finishPrompt: Click the button after watching the entire lesson to claim your rewards!
lesson.successDesc: You have successfully completed the lesson and earned the following rewards:

learnViews.astronomyDesc: Learn the secrets of stars, galaxies, and the universe
learnViews.physicsDesc: Learn all topics in physics — from kinematics to nuclear physics
learnViews.creativityDesc: Cosmic art, design, and creative thinking
learnViews.interviewsDesc: Exclusive interviews with scientists, astronauts, and experts
learnViews.problemsDesc: Total {count} problems — solve each one and strengthen your knowledge
learnViews.correctDesc: Congratulations! You have successfully solved this problem.
learnViews.wrongDesc: Don't worry, knowledge grows through effort.
learnViews.keepGoing: Keep going, you will achieve great results!
learnViews.lessonDesc1: In this part you will get acquainted with the basic concepts, laws and important formulas of {lessonName}. Watch the video lesson and consolidate your knowledge.
learnViews.lessonDesc2: Examples of solving practical problems on {lessonName}. In this video we will look at how to apply the basic rules in practice and analyze problems of varying complexity.
learnViews.lessonDesc3: In-depth theoretical knowledge on the topic. We will observe the phenomena of {lessonName} using experiments and visual animations. This will help you understand the rules more deeply.
learnViews.lessonDesc4: Analysis of complex problems and test questions. We will learn about the most common mistakes on {lessonName} and how to avoid them. Special recommendations for exam preparation.
learnViews.lessonDesc5: The final part to consolidate the topic {lessonName}. We will summarize all the knowledge learned in previous parts and look at its real-life applications.
learnViews.lessonDescription: Explore the engineering and design principles behind {name}. Watch the video to understand its role in space exploration and development.
